title: Overview
text:
This convention leverages Shiny’s built-in module functionality to organize application files and code to make developing, updating, and collaborating on apps easier than the traditional single-file method. These modules are not to be confused with Python’s native modules, these are designed specifically for the Shiny framework.
Each UI layout has top-level navigation tabs (either in a navbar or a sidebar) to allow the user to navigate the various pages within the application. For each navigation tab, there will be a separate Shiny module and each module will have its own separate R file. Each file will contain only its associated page’s UI and server code.
More here: Shiny Modules

title: Libraries
text:
Any 3rd party libraries not included in the lists below should be approved before adding them to a Shiny application. This is to ensure that it is distributed via the proper channels (e.g. CRAN) and is well maintained by the developer.
Try to minimize the libraries used in each app to the minimum needed to perform its intended purpose. This is to minimize the overhead of maintaining multiple package versions to avoid conflicts. Additionally, it reduces the amount of packages a user has to install or update if the app is shared locally.
See below for commonly used libraries:

Front-End
shiny
DT
bslib
ggplot2
plotly
leaflet
shinycssloaders
shinyalert



Back-End
Base R
dplyr
dbplyr
DBI
odbc
data.table
lubridate
stringr
purrr
readxl
writexl
vroom
dataRetrieval
sf

title: Folder Structure
text:
app.R
The folder structure starts by creating an app.R file in the root directory. This is the entry point to the app that imports the core UI and server components. You then create an R/ (R) subdirectory where each page file will be created.


R/
UI and server code should go in the R/ subdirectory. Shiny will automatically source all .R files in the this subdirectory, so you do not need to import them in the app.R file.


_global.R
Global variables shared across the entire application should go in a _global.R file. However, if data is loaded here or outside of a module or function’s scope, it might slow down your application at startup. It’s better practice to load data within module or function scope.


data/
Data files should go in a data/ subdirectory.


www/
Any custom HTML/CSS/JavaScript files should go in the www/ subdirectory.


Tree view
project-directory/
    app.R
    R/
    data/
    www/
More here: Shiny App Formats

title: Code Structure
text:
In the app file, you only define top-level pages in the nav object and only top-level modules in the server function.

State Object
The pages in your application will most likely need to access reactive values from other pages and be able to react to those values’ changes. For example, a user input page filtering data on a table output in another page. Instead of passing multiple separate reactive values to each page module to achieve this, there will be a single state object that holds each reactive value that will be passed to each page module in the app file.
Additionally, each module can have its own internal state object that other pages cannot access. These are initialized at the start of the page’s server function. This is optional, as Shiny’s standard reactive workflow works the same way here.
In R, state is created by using Shiny’s reactiveValues function:
ui <- navbarPage(
    title = "Shiny for R",
    id = "app_id",

    # Call UI modules in each tab panel
    tabPanel("Page 1", page1UI(NS("page_1"))),
    navbarMenu(
        title = "Nav Dropdown",
        tabPanel("Page 2", page2UI(NS("page_2"))),
    )
)

server <- function(input, output, session){
    # Initialize global state object
    state <- reactiveValues()
    
    # Pass state to each module
    page1Module("page_1", state)
    page2Module("page_2", state)
}


Custom Functions
Functions you write that don’t depend on or affect the UI or server, such as for data processing, should be separated from the server code entirely. Your app should capture any reactive values within the server code, then pass the non-reactive value to your function. The function should then return another non-reactive value which can then be handled within the server/UI code.
Place such functions in a separate functions.R file. This makes it easier to test them outside of a Shiny session. Try not to write custom functions solely for UI functionality. This creates a dependency within your application that is hard to test and can become quickly outdated. Prefer to repeat UI/server code if necessary.

title: Shiny Template for R
text: This document provides a convention for developing web applications using POSIT’s Shiny framework.

title: Naming Conventions
text:
Certain conventions should be followed when implementing this structure so that the app can be easily collaborated on by other developers.

R code
For general code formatting, follow the Tidyverse Style Guide.


Shiny code
For Shiny specifically, see below:
Top-level UI modules: The name of the page with the ui suffix in camelCase (myUI)
Top-level server modules: The name of the page with the module suffix in camelCase (myModule)
Global state object: Named state in both the module function call and body
Optional module state: Named module
